Money follows geopolitics, and it just told you where it's going for a decade. Good. Let's do the tape. Market overview. Market Overview Thursday's close, and it's records. S&P 500 up 81 basis points to 7,543.64. Nasdaq Composite up a full 1.3 percent to 26,206.89. Dow up 27 basis points to 52,487.41. Russell 2000 up to 2,992.54. VIX pinned at 15.9. And the leadership is the whole point. This wasn't broad — it was semiconductors. Memory, specifically. The semis ETF, SMH, up 2.5 percent. Micron up 4.5, SanDisk up 7.6. The HBM complex is the engine of this tape, jolted by the SK Hynix listing we'll get to. When people say the AI trade's defensive right now — look at the tape. It's the offense. But under the equity records, the bond market is telling a different story. Yields backed up. That's the reflation tell, and the crux of the day. The 10-year's at 4.54, up from 4.48 Monday. Two-year 4.16, 30-year above 5. Fed funds held at 3.5 to 3.75 a fourth straight meeting. June's disinflation trade — cheaper oil, lower yields, a Fed with cover to cut — has quietly reversed. And the minutes made it official. Walk me through the dots, because this is a genuine regime shift. The June minutes dropped Wednesday and they're a different Fed. Nine of eighteen participants now see at least one hike before year-end. Eight, no change. Exactly one sees a cut. The dovish dissent that dominated the spring has vanished — a few even flagged a case for hiking in June. That's a 9-to-1 skew toward hiking versus cutting. Six weeks ago the debate was how many cuts. And it maps onto everything else — oil firming, China reflating, core services sticky. The wrinkle is Warsh himself: the new chair submitted no dot and ran a roughly 130-word statement. He's deliberately telling you nothing, which forces the market to trade the committee's distribution, not the chair's guidance. Opacity as a policy tool. Which brings us to the loudest instrument in the room. Gold. Gold around 4,116, silver near 60. That's off January's spike above 5,300, so not a fresh record — but structurally extraordinary, and bid for reasons that rhyme with the whole show. The PBoC bought a 19th straight month. A record 45 percent of official reserve managers say they intend to add. And the deficit-and-debasement trade is the backdrop — JPMorgan's still carrying a 5,000 target. So gold and AI-semis, both bid. Frame the barbell, because that's the throughline in one picture. Exactly. When the market smells reflation it doesn't sell — it rotates into what can't be printed and what compounds faster than inflation. Hard assets on one end, the AI-memory buildout on the other. Gold's the debasement hedge; the semis are the growth hedge. Same trade, two faces. What gets sold is the middle — long-duration nominal bonds — and that's your yields backing up. Rounding out the board — dollar and oil. Dollar firm. Euro at 1.143, near the year's low. Dollar-yen at 161.8, near the year's high — yen still weak despite the BOJ at 1 percent, because the rate differential is what matters. Dollar index around 100.6. Oil we covered — Brent 76.5, WTI 72.2. US business. Two, and the first is the biggest listing on the tape in years. U.S. Business SK Hynix went public on the Nasdaq Thursday, ticker SKHY. They priced 177.9 million ADRs at 149 dollars — a 3 percent premium to the Korean close — raising about 26.5 billion. That's one of the largest US listings ever by a foreign company, at a market cap around 1.1 trillion. And the book was ludicrous. Seven times oversubscribed? About seven times, Baillie Gifford and Coatue anchoring. And the reason is one number: SK Hynix holds roughly 58 percent of the global high-bandwidth-memory market, and ranks one or two in DRAM, HBM and NAND. US institutions paying up for the purest direct claim on the memory supercycle that exists. So this isn't a de-risking market shy of AI. This is the market writing a 26-billion-dollar check to get more of it. That's the tell. The debate for a month was whether the AI trade was overextended. The order book for the single cleanest AI-memory name says the marginal institutional dollar disagrees — hard. It's why the whole semis complex traded the way it did into the print. Deal two — and it's a real-economy one. Kroger. Kroger's buying Giant Eagle for 1.65 billion — 1.25 in cash plus about 400 million of assumed liabilities. Giant Eagle's a family-owned Ohio and Pennsylvania grocer, roughly 9 billion in sales, 197 supermarkets and 11 pharmacies. Kroger got burned trying to buy Albertsons. Why does this one clear when that one didn't? Footprint. The overlap here is limited, so the antitrust math is far friendlier than the Albertsons overlap that sank the last attempt. They've signaled some divestitures to get it through. The National Grocers Association is pushing back for the independents, and it won't close before 2027 — but this is the achievable version of the deal Kroger wanted. And the signal underneath it. Same one we keep hitting — when organic growth is scarce, boards buy it. Late-cycle consolidation, and the pipeline keeps filling. Two international to close the block. International Business & Politics The ECB. The July ninth meeting accounts defended June's hike to a 2.25 percent deposit rate as, quote, robust across scenarios, with staff seeing euro-area inflation averaging 3 percent this year. So Europe is doing exactly what the Fed minutes just hinted at — tightening into the reflation, not easing out of a slowdown. That's the synchronization that matters. A year ago the central-bank story was a coordinated cutting cycle. Now the ECB's hiked, the BOJ's at 1, the Fed's dots point up, and even the Philippine central bank's on hold after hiking. The whole policy world has pivoted from supporting growth to guarding against inflation. A regime, not a data point. Second story — France, and it's a political-risk one. Marine Le Pen says she'll contest the 2027 presidency despite being handed a court-ordered electronic monitor this week for embezzlement. So the far-right standard-bearer runs from under a legal cloud. And the market expression is the sovereign spread. The OAT-Bund spread is the fear gauge. French political risk plus an already-strained fiscal position is the cocktail that widens it — a slow-burn re-rating, not a gap. But with European rates rising for inflation reasons too, it's the story to keep on the radar. China to close the block. China This is the cleanest read on the whole reflation thread, and it flips a narrative we've been running for months. China's producer prices rose 4.1 percent year-on-year in June — the strongest since July 2022, and a fourth straight monthly gain. For a country that was the poster child for exported deflation, that is a genuine turn. What's driving it? Two things, and they're different in kind. One is cyclical — firmer global commodities feeding through, production materials up 5.5, mining prices up 16.5. The other is policy, and it's the interesting one: Beijing's anti-involution campaign — a deliberate crackdown on cut-throat, capacity-glut price wars that were manufacturing deflation. So the state is engineering pricing power by taking capacity out. That's supply discipline by decree. And that's the second-order read for the world. China exporting deflation was a global disinflationary anchor for years. If Beijing reflates factory prices to protect margins, that anchor lifts — and everyone importing Chinese goods imports a little more inflation. The catch: consumer prices rose just 1 percent, and fell on the month. Factory-gate reflation with still-soft domestic demand — margins caught in the middle. Producers squeezed, consumers quiet, but the deflation trade is over. The deflation trade is over. And that's the through-thread — reflation showing up in Chinese PPI, in oil, in the Fed's dots, and in the price of gold, all at once. Changing gears. Energy / Data Centers / Utilities Segment two — the energy desk. And the story's moved, Alex. For months it was the interconnection queue. Now it's something else. The off-grid pivot has gone mainstream. Hyperscalers stopped waiting on the queue, and in three weeks a cluster of behind-the-meter self-supply deals crossed the tape. Start with Chevron and Microsoft — Project Kilby, in Pecos, Texas: a 2-gigawatt AI campus paired with a 20-year, up-to-2.67-gigawatt gas plant built behind the meter, GE Vernova turbines, first power 2028. Behind the meter — it never touches the public grid, so the interconnection queue is irrelevant to them. Precisely, and it's not a one-off. Blue Energy and GE Vernova signed a 2.5-gigawatt gas-plus-nuclear hybrid in Texas — two 7HA turbines for a gigawatt by 2030, ramping toward 1.5 via BWRX-300 small modular reactors by 2032. Kodiak and Baker Hughes, a turbine framework, a gigawatt by 2030 with a path to 1.8. Tallgrass and Mitsubishi, about 1,150 megawatts at the Cheyenne Power Hub in Wyoming. All inside three weeks. So if the demand is landing and the money is committed, where's the actual bottleneck now? It's migrated — off the grid and onto the turbine floor. Siemens Energy said at end-June it's booked 14 gigawatts of gas-turbine orders this year, about 60 percent for data centers. GE Vernova, Siemens and Mitsubishi are effectively sold out into 2029, 2030. Transformer lead times are 3 to 5 years. The scarce asset is the machine that makes the power, not the wire that carries it. Which completely reframes the investable surface. The long isn't the utility anymore. That's the second-order read. The cleanest long is the OEM oligopoly — the three companies that physically build the turbines and reactors, sold-out books and pricing power. The risk is to the regulated utilities, because their biggest prospective loads are defecting behind the meter. That's disintermediation — the load-growth thesis everyone bought the utilities for partly walks out the door. It even undercuts the FERC interconnection reform that only kicked off June eighteenth. And there's a ratepayer angle that ties straight back to our inflation theme. Oregon's regulator just approved a roughly 29 percent rate increase for the data-center customer class, which actually cuts residential bills about 1.3 percent — the first concrete carve-out that says data centers pay for data-center power. The politics of power-bill inflation are the ballot-box counterweight to this whole buildout, and it feeds the reflation print through the electricity line. One myth to kill before we move on — the viral claim that half of this year's build got canceled. SemiAnalysis put that down on July second — traced it to an AI-written false alarm, pegged the actual forecast cut at about 1 percent. Meta alone locked more than 5 gigawatts this half. The buildout's intact — the only thing that changed is where the choke point sits. Bring it home. Philippines Segment three — the Philippines. Weather lead, then the macro bind. Typhoon Inday — international name Bavi — entered as a super typhoon on July seventh, since weakened to around 150 kilometers an hour. Good news domestically: it's staying offshore, exits today or tomorrow, makes landfall on mainland China by Sunday. Highest was Signal Number 2 over Batanes. So the wind misses, but the rain doesn't. The rain's the story — the enhanced habagat, the southwest monsoon. PAGASA had Heavy Rainfall Warning Number 4 up, gutter-deep flooding hit Manila on the fourth, MMDA went to blue alert. A rainfall-and-flood event, not a landfall one — and it still hits the food-logistics chain at the margin. And the central bank is boxed in exactly the way we flagged. Where does BSP sit now? On hold at 4.75, after a June nineteenth hike — its second straight. And the June print perfectly illustrates the bind: headline eased to 6.4 percent from 6.8, which sounds like relief, but core inflation hit a 31-month high. The underlying trend's still hot even as the headline cools. Headline down, core up — that's the worst combination for a central banker trying to look through it. It is, and they're not looking through it. BSP actually raised its 2026 inflation forecast to 6.4 percent, citing the Middle-East oil and fertilizer shocks by name. It's our reflation thread landing on a specific emerging market — the same firmer oil rebuilding the war premium is widening the Philippine import bill. And the currency is the forcing function. Peso's at 61.4 to 61.6, record-low territory — it first broke 61 in April, and analysts now see 63 by year-end. Firmer oil plus broad dollar strength widens the import bill and stokes imported inflation, and the bank can't let that pass-through get disorderly. And the offset that used to cushion it is fading — cash remittances grew just 2 percent in April, the slowest in nearly four years. So the structural shock absorber is softening right as the cyclical pressure builds. That's the uncomfortable version. Growth's intact, the PSEi's around 6,220 after a decent run — but on the macro, it's a textbook imported-inflation squeeze with a weaker safety net. Onward. Vocab Of The Day Segment four — vocab of the day. Two Mandarin, then two Tagalog, each tied to today's stories, all conducted in the language. Mandarin first, Alex. 第一个词是一个连词:一旦。意思是 once, 或者 in the event that。 (The first word is a connective: yīdàn — "once," or "in the event that.") 一旦。 这个词和"如果"有什么区别? (Yīdàn. How is it different from rúguǒ, "if"?) 问得好。"如果"是中性的假设,但"一旦"强调一个决定性的转折点,常常是你不希望发生的事。比如今天的头条:一旦停火协议破裂,油价就会反弹。 (Good question. Rúguǒ is a neutral hypothetical, but yīdàn stresses a decisive turning point, often something you'd rather avoid. Take today's headline: "Once the ceasefire collapses, oil prices will rebound.") 所以搭配是"一旦……就……"。一旦市场恐慌,资金就流向黄金。 (So the collocation is yīdàn… jiù… "Once the market panics, money flows into gold.") 完全正确,这是书面语和分析里的高频结构。 (Exactly right — it's a high-frequency structure in written and analytical language.) 第二个词很适合金融:贬值。意思是 to depreciate, to lose value。 (The second word is perfect for finance: biǎnzhí — "to depreciate," "to lose value.") 贬值。 比索创了新低,可以说"比索贬值"吗? (Biǎnzhí. The peso hit a new low — can I say "the peso is depreciating"?) 可以,货币贬值是标准搭配。注意跟"掉价"的区别:"贬值"是正式的、宏观的——货币、资产的价值下降;"掉价"是口语,指东西便宜了,或者一个人"掉价"、丢面子。 (Yes — "currency depreciation" is the standard collocation. Note the contrast with diàojià: biǎnzhí is formal and macro — a currency or asset losing value; diàojià is colloquial, a thing getting cheaper, or a person "losing face.") 明白了。黄金上涨,正是因为大家担心货币贬值。 (Got it. Gold is rising precisely because everyone worries about currency debasement.) 说得漂亮。这就是今天的主线。 (Beautifully put — that's today's throughline.) Now Tagalog — and for native speakers, so we go for nuance, not glossary. First word, Alex. Ang unang salita ay "unos." Isang biglaang bagyo o sigwa — pero madalas ginagamit sa krisis. (The first word is "unos" — a sudden storm or squall, but often used for a crisis.) Ano ang pagkakaiba ng "unos" sa "bagyo"? (What's the difference between unos and bagyo?) Ang "bagyo" ay ang literal na typhoon, tulad ni Inday ngayon. Ang "unos" ay ang biglaang dagok — pwede mong sabihin, "unos sa merkado," ang biglaang pagbagsak ng presyo. At may "sigwa" din — kadalasan, pampulitika iyon, tulad ng Sigwa ng Unang Sangkatauhan. (Bagyo is the literal typhoon, like Inday today. Unos is the sudden onslaught — you can say "an unos in the market," a sudden collapse in prices. And there's also sigwa — usually political, like the First Quarter Storm.) Kaya "unos" para sa merkado, "bagyo" para sa panahon. Malinaw. (So unos for the market, bagyo for the weather. Clear.) Tama. Ikalawang salita: "kagipitan." Ang ibig sabihin — a tight spot, financial straits. (Right. Second word: kagipitan — a tight spot, financial straits.) Parang "hirap"? (Like hirap, "hardship"?) Malapit, pero mas tiyak. Ang "hirap" ay pangkalahatan. Ang "kagipitan" ay ang kalagayan ng pagkakagipit — halimbawa, ang kagipitan ng BSP sa pagitan ng implasyon at piso, o ang kagipitan ng bawat pamilya sa mahal na bilihin. Galing sa "gipit" — cornered, hard-up. (Close, but more specific. Hirap is general. Kagipitan is the state of being squeezed — for instance, the BSP's bind between inflation and the peso, or every family's squeeze under high prices. It comes from gipit — cornered, hard-up.) Kaya ang buong palabas natin ngayon — puno ng kagipitan at unos. (So our whole show today — full of squeeze and storms.) Iyan ang wika sa loob ng balita. Language living inside the news. That's the idea. Art / Pop Segment five — art and pop, quick. A proper rock day. The Rolling Stones drop "Foreign Tongues" today — billed as their 25th British studio album — and Jack White puts out "Frozen Charlotte" the same morning. Two of the last guitar lifers landing on the same Friday. And the pop side? Madonna went back to the well — "Confessions II," a full sequel to 2005's "Confessions on a Dance Floor," reuniting with producer Stuart Price. And Ariana Grande's post-"Wicked" return, "Petal," is set for the 31st. The screen side? A rougher story — legacy-IP fatigue is in the numbers. Disney's live-action "Moana" opens today tracking soft — 45 to 65 against an early 85-million call, on a budget near 250, a 33 on Rotten Tomatoes. That's a projection, not a result. And "Minions and Monsters" already posted a franchise-low 61-million opening over July fourth. Anything cutting the other way? Nolan. "The Odyssey" lands July seventeenth tracking 100 to 120 domestic — original-scale spectacle still pulls. And Phillips booked 507 million in first-half auction sales, up around 60 percent, led by watches. The high end's intact even as the multiplex wobbles. Trending Meme Segment six — the meme, and we report it, we don't perform it. The runaway sound of the summer is a 49-second track called "Bangladesh" by an American artist, Ian McConnell — the line everyone's using is "you never take me to Bangladesh." Released early June, and it's everywhere on TikTok. What makes that one travel? Two things. It opens on the hook and loops cleanly, so it's frictionless as an audio. And the lyric starts as a sincere relationship complaint, then escalates into absurd demands — which hands everyone a plug-and-play template: "you never" plus something ridiculous. That's the engine of any format that spreads: low effort to remix. And the co-sign wave? McConnell's past 300,000 monthly Spotify listeners, Lizzo cut her own version, and SZA and Chance the Rapper have both engaged. When the establishment artists adopt the bit, you know it's crossed from niche to mainstream. This summer's answer to the spring's "saxophone gets louder" formats. From a niche song to a universal template. Good cue for the last one. One Good Thing And before we let you go — one good thing to end on. After a week of typhoons and heat waves, here's a hopeful one that fits the theme. The Gulf of Aqaba reef — up in the northern Red Sea, off Jordan — was put forward this month for UNESCO World Heritage status. And the reason it's special is the reason it matters: its corals are unusually heat-resilient. They shrug off water temperatures that bleach and kill reefs almost everywhere else on Earth. So it's a natural climate refuge — a reef that's essentially pre-adapted to the warming that's coming. That's exactly it. Scientists think it could be one of the last great reef systems standing at the end of the century — which makes protecting it a strategic act, not just a conservation nicety. A reef built to take the heat, getting the recognition to keep it safe. A reef that's essentially built to outlast the warming. We'll take it. That's your commute. Whatever the day throws at you — — take it easy, look after each other, and we'll be right back here tomorrow morning. See you then. 一旦 once; in the event that; as soon as Iran ceasefire collapse / oil war premium 贬值 to depreciate; to devalue; to lose value peso record low / gold debasement trade unos a sudden violent storm or squall; figuratively, a crisis or turmoil Typhoon Inday / the reflation squall in markets kagipitan a tight spot; financial straits; an acute squeeze BSP policy bind / peso & import-bill squeeze

docs.title: Thursday, July 9, 2026
docs.text: Thursday, July 9, 2026 Cold Open Good morning, this is The Morning Commute. Thursday, July ninth. I'm Sam. And I'm Alex. The throughline today is the war premium coming back. Two weeks ago the story was cheaper oil and lower yields doing the Fed's work — well, that just went into reverse. The Iran ceasefire collapsed overnight, oil spiked more than 5%, Treasury yields are backing up, and the disinflation trade everyone leaned into is unwinding. The twist that keeps this from being a straight risk-off day: the AI buildout underneath got louder, not quieter — the chip complex actually led while the rest of the tape flinched. Headlines Headlines. World desk, and there's only one place to start — Iran, because it's the engine under everything today. It is the whole engine. The June ceasefire is, in the President's own word, "over." He said it flat out at the NATO summit in Ankara, and the US resumed strikes on Iran on Wednesday — Central Command framed it as degrading Iran's ability to threaten freedom of navigation through the Strait of Hormuz. Iran's Revolutionary Guards say they retaliated against two US bases in Kuwait and two in Bahrain. And Tehran is threatening to close Hormuz entirely — a chokepoint that has historically moved around 20 million barrels a day. So the framework everyone was pricing as durable two weeks ago has fully broken. What actually lit the fuse? Three vessels were attacked in or near Hormuz on Tuesday. Iran called the US strikes a gross violation of the memorandum of understanding the two sides signed last month; Washington says the vessel attacks were the violation. The point for markets is the direction of travel — the war premium that bled out of crude in June is going straight back in, and the tail everyone dismissed, a Hormuz closure, is suddenly back on the board. And unlike June, this time the market can't just look through it. Right — in June the move was disinflationary and the market loved it. Now it's the opposite vector: higher oil, higher yields, tighter financial conditions. Same variable, crude, running the other way. Second world story, and it's the other headline out of the same summit — NATO in Ankara wrapped up, and Erdogan called it historic. The deliverable: the US will license Ukraine to produce Patriot interceptor missiles domestically, and allies pledged 70 billion euros in military aid to Ukraine for 2026, reaffirming Article 5. A Patriot production license is a real escalation in capability, not just another aid tranche. It's a structural shift — you're handing Kyiv the ability to make its own air defense rather than rationing exports from a thin global stock. On the wire, two more real-but-adjacent items: Trump reopened the Greenland question — the Danish PM's line was "Greenland is of course not for sale" — and Russia banned diesel exports after Ukrainian drone strikes on its refineries triggered fuel shortages and hours-long queues. That last one feeds straight back into the oil story. Good place to pivot to the tape, because the tape is all about that oil-and-yields reversal. Market Overview Wednesday's close: the S&P off a tenth — sorry, off about three-tenths of a percent to seventy-four eighty-two, twenty-one points lighter. The Dow was the carnage — down five hundred seventy-seven points, more than a full percent, to fifty-two-three-forty-eight. But the Nasdaq actually finished green, up two-tenths to twenty-five-eight-seventy. So a violent split under the surface. And that split is the whole story. The Dow down a percent while the Nasdaq holds green — that's the mirror image of the rotation we described two weeks ago. Then it was value taking the baton from tech. Now? Now it's the reverse handoff. Rising yields and a fresh oil shock punish the cyclicals and rate-sensitives that dominate the Dow, while the semiconductor complex — the purest AI-buildout expression — actually rallied and dragged the Nasdaq up against the geopolitical tape. Chip stocks lifted the index while everything rate-sensitive got sold. Quantify the yields move, because that's the transmission belt. The ten-year sits at four-fifty-six, up from four-forty-eight on Monday — call it eight basis points in three sessions, and grinding higher. The thirty-year is back above 5%, at five-oh-six. Two-year at four-twenty-one. This isn't a growth-scare rally in bonds — it's the opposite, a yields-up move driven by the oil shock and a Fed that just told us it's more worried about inflation than the market wanted to hear. Put the crude numbers on it, since that's the input. Brent settled at seventy-eight-oh-two, up more than 5% on the session, and it traded as high as eighty-fifty-nine intraday — up nearly 9% at the peak before it came in. US WTI settled around seventy-three fifty-two, up four-and-a-half percent. Two weeks ago Brent was in the low seventies with the war premium out; one collapsed ceasefire later, it's knocking on 80. And you can see the pain in the rate-sensitive corners. Vividly — and it's the exact inverse of the KB Home trade we flagged in June. Then, a falling ten-year lit a fire under homebuilders. Now mortgage rates are jumping again as the bond market sells off, adding hundreds of dollars a month for a prospective buyer, and the whole affordability complex reprices the other way. The ten-year is the affordability lever, and it just moved against housing. So how do you frame the Fed setup now, versus the constructive read we had? Materially more hawkish, and not because of the market — because of the Fed itself. The June minutes dropped Wednesday, and they were a genuine hawkish surprise. More on that in the energy block, because the reason is partly an energy and AI story. But the top line: risks to inflation tilted to the upside, and a chair in Warsh who is leaning into price stability over cuts. And the volatility read? This is the tell — equity vol stayed contained, the VIX around 16, but rates vol is where the action is; the MOVE index ticked up. Same lesson as June, just inverted: the whole move is being driven by the bond market, so watch rates vol, not the VIX. If yields keep backing up, the pressure on the broad tape builds even if the chip names keep leading. One more cross-asset read before we leave the tape — where did the haven bid actually go? It scrambled, and that's the uncomfortable part. In the classic playbook you buy long Treasuries when the world gets scary — but you can't hide in the thirty-year when the thirty-year is the thing selling off. So the safety bid fractured: into the front end of the curve, into gold, into cash. When the asset that's supposed to protect you is itself the source of the shock, the correlations that normally cushion a portfolio stop cushioning it. That's what makes a yields-up, oil-up day genuinely different from an ordinary risk-off. So there was nowhere clean to hide. US business — lead with the biggest strategic story of the day. U.S. Business Apple and Broadcom, and it's a landmark. Apple committed more than 30 billion dollars to Broadcom to design and manufacture custom silicon and advanced wireless components in the United States — radio-frequency parts, the FBAR filters, the connectivity stack. The deal runs through 2031, is expected to yield more than 15 billion US-made chips, and it's the single largest commitment yet under Apple's American Manufacturing Program. So this is the onshoring thesis getting a marquee number attached. What's the read past the headline? Two reads. One, it's real capacity — Broadcom is putting 1.5 billion dollars into expanding its Fort Collins, Colorado plant to build this. Two, it's political architecture: this sits inside Apple's roughly 600-billion-dollar, four-year US investment pledge. On a day when the macro story is a foreign oil shock, Apple is buying supply-chain insurance at home — and the market read it as a semiconductor-demand signal, which is part of why chips led. The AI buildout showing up in a consumer-electronics supply chain. And the second US story is the other end of the economy — the consumer. The consumer read is PepsiCo. Q2 net revenue grew six-point-four percent, organic growth two-point-four percent, and the story is pricing power holding up even as North America food and beverage volumes stay under pressure. It's the classic late-cycle staples signal: they're still getting price, but the volume tells you the household is stretched. And that matters more than usual on a day the inflation impulse is turning back up. Exactly the tension. If oil's climbing and the Fed's worried about sticky inflation, a staples giant leaning on price rather than volume is the microcosm — corporate margins defended through pricing, household budgets absorbing it. That's the disinflation reversal at the kitchen-table level, one earnings call early. Two international stories, and they both route through the same energy-and-rates plumbing. International Business & Politics Start with Japan, because it's flashing a warning the whole rates complex should be watching. Prime Minister Takaichi's push for more spending and tax cuts is keeping the yen sinking — dollar-yen is up near its highs for the year — even as Japanese long-bond yields have climbed to a multi-decade high. Barron's called it a coded warning for US stocks, and they're right to. Unpack why a Japanese bond yield is a US equity problem. Because Japan is the world's great low-yield anchor and the funding leg of the global carry trade. When thirty-year JGB yields push to levels not seen in a generation, the incentive for Japanese capital to stay home rises, and the cheap-yen funding that quietly underwrites risk assets everywhere gets more expensive. It's June's yen puzzle, advanced — the BOJ tightening cycle plus a fiscally expansive government is exactly the mix that eventually forces a carry unwind, and that never stays contained in Tokyo. Second international story — Russia, and it's an oil-throughline story. It is. Russia banned diesel exports after a systematic campaign of Ukrainian drone strikes on its refineries triggered domestic gasoline shortages and long queues at the pump. Take a major exporter's diesel off the seaborne market at the same moment Hormuz risk is spiking, and you've got a distillate-tightness story stacking on top of a crude story. Two supply shocks rhyming. So both international stories are really the same story — supply getting squeezed while demand for energy is structurally rising. That's the synthesis, and it's the bridge to the energy block. One quick market coda: even into this, Morgan Stanley says global M&A is on track for a record 6.4 trillion dollars this year, eclipsing the 2021 boom. Buoyant equity markets and corporate confidence — the animal spirits haven't read the geopolitics memo. China to close the headlines block. China China's AI labs are raising eye-watering sums. Zhipu AI is looking to raise around 4 billion dollars, and DeepSeek pulled in roughly 7 billion last month — both racing to compete with America's chip giants and hyperscalers on open-weight models. This is the self-reliance push with a capital structure attached. The second-order read is that export controls were supposed to starve exactly this. Multi-billion-dollar raises say the ceiling is higher than intended. That's the lens. The controls throttle the best silicon, but they don't throttle capital or algorithmic ingenuity — and open-weight models are a deliberate strategy to win mindshare and set standards even while compute-constrained. It advances the DeepSeek-moment theme from a few weeks back: not a one-off benchmark, a funded, sustained campaign. And it sits on the same two-speed economy we keep flagging. Precisely — world-class AI ambition on one side, soft domestic demand and a negative GDP deflator on the other. The compute-and-capital race they're contesting hard; the demand problem at home they're not solving. But make no mistake — a Chinese lab raising 7 billion is a data point in the same global story as Apple's 30-billion-dollar chip deal. The whole planet is capitalizing the same buildout. Which is the perfect handoff. Take us to the energy desk. Energy / Data Centers / Utilities Segment two — energy, data centers and the grid. And today it's not a sidebar to the macro; the Fed put it at the center of the macro. Alex. That's the marker of the day. The June Fed minutes, released Wednesday, explicitly said strong demand for AI infrastructure would likely sustain upward pressure on prices for technology products — and for electricity. Read that again: the Federal Reserve is now naming AI-driven power demand as an inflation input, right next to the energy shock. The minutes put inflation risks tilted to the upside, and that hawkish read is a big reason yields backed up. So the thing we've called the counterweight to disinflation just got institutional cover from the central bank. It graduated from podcast thesis to FOMC language. And the supply side is scrambling to meet it in the least clean way possible — onsite gas. SemiAnalysis has been all over this: OpenAI and Oracle placed the largest onsite gas-generation order ever, a 2.3-gigawatt plant in Texas. xAI has already deployed more than 500 megawatts of gas turbines next to its data centers, and Solaris expects over 1.1 gigawatts running for xAI by the second quarter of 2027. They're not waiting for the grid. They're building their own power stations. Because they can't wait. The interconnection queue is the binding constraint. In Texas, tens of gigawatts of data-center load requests pour in every month, and over the past year barely more than a single gigawatt has been approved — the grid is effectively sold out. So hyperscalers go behind-the-meter with dedicated gas, and the equipment makers are the bottleneck: Caterpillar is doubling engine output and roughly two-and-a-half-times-ing turbine production between 2024 and 2030, and Bloom Energy says it can hit 2 gigawatts a year of fuel-cell capacity by the end of 2026. Put the demand curve in numbers so people feel the slope. US AI power demand was around 3 gigawatts in 2023. It's over 28 gigawatts now — a roughly ten-fold jump in three years. And the capital behind it: the top five hyperscalers alone are on track for about 602 billion dollars of capex this year, up 36% year-on-year, while global data-center capex is running north of 726 billion. That's the demand line that just won't quit. And the ratepayer question — the one that turns this political. That's the live wire, and there was a concrete example on the tape Wednesday. ComEd energized two new 345-kilovolt transmission substations in Illinois, unlocking up to 550 megawatts of wind. Good news for supply — but every one of those upgrades has a cost, and when a household sees its bill rise to fund grid expansion built for hyperscaler load, the cross-subsidization fight goes straight to the statehouse. The investable surface is regulated utilities with the rate base and the turbine makers; the political surface is who pays. And it's not only the hyperscalers competing for those electrons anymore. That's the piece almost nobody prices. Toyota just committed 3.6 billion dollars to expand its San Antonio campus in Texas, and the subtext is power and proximity — you site heavy manufacturing where you can actually get interconnection. Cheap, firm power used to be a footnote in a site-selection deck; it's now line one. So the same grid math that governs a data center now governs a car plant, and every new industrial load competes for the same transformers, turbines and queue slots the AI campuses are fighting over. So the grid is quietly redrawing the map of where things get built. And feeding the exact inflation channel the Fed just named. Scarcity in transformers and turbines pushes the delivered price of power up for everyone on the system — the data center, the factory, and the household on the same feeder. It's why this is a structural tightener, not a one-quarter shock: the demand is a decade long, and the supply chain to serve it is measured in years. And the through-line to the AI trade wobble — because the market's relationship with this buildout is complicated. This is the paradox in one stock. Nvidia's forward price-to-earnings just fell to about 22 times — a multi-year low — even though it is the single most central company to everything I just described. The demand is roaring, the capex is roaring, and the market is de-rating the marquee name. That's the throughline in miniature: the AI trade can wobble on positioning and rates while the AI buildout underneath it accelerates. Two different clocks. One's a sentiment clock, one's a concrete-and-copper clock. And the concrete-and-copper clock only speeds up. Which is why, even on a day the oil shock is stealing headlines, the electricity story is the more durable one — it's a decade-long demand shock the Fed is now writing into its inflation outlook. Cheaper crude was the June relief; pricier power is the standing structural tightener underneath it all. Take it to the Philippines — where both of today's threats, the geopolitical one and a literal storm, are landing at once. Philippines Segment three — the Philippines. Weather lead, then the macro, and the macro ties right back to the oil reversal. Weather first, and there's a new system. Super Typhoon Bavi — local name Inday — entered the Philippine area of responsibility Wednesday morning and was downgraded to a typhoon before dawn Thursday. Maximum sustained winds around 175 kilometers an hour, gusts to 215. The relief: it is not expected to make landfall. But it's dragging in the habagat, the southwest monsoon, and that's the real story for daily life. So a near-miss on the wind, but a soaking regardless. Exactly the June pattern, new storm. PAGASA has heavy-rainfall warnings across the western half of the country — Antique, Negros Occidental, Palawan, Iloilo, Occidental Mindoro, down into Lanao and Sarangani — with the enhanced monsoon set to drench the west from the eighth through the tenth. The center stays offshore; the rain does not spare anyone. Now the macro bind, and it's tighter than it was two weeks ago because of our lead story. Much tighter. The BSP is at four-and-three-quarters percent after a second straight hike in June, with inflation still seen averaging six-point-four percent this year, and not back inside the two-to-four-percent target band until 2027. Two weeks ago the hopeful read was that falling oil would let them stop. The Iran collapse just took that hope off the table. Spell that out — because in late June the story was oil relief giving them breathing room. It was, and this is the cruel reversal. The Philippines imports its oil, so a Brent move from the low seventies back toward 80 feeds straight into food and transport inflation and widens the import bill. The relief that looked imminent is gone; the imported-inflation pressure is back. Analysts were already split — Capital Economics saw one more hike to 5% in August, Pantheon thought June might be the last. This oil spike tilts that argument back toward one more hike. And the peso is the forcing function, as always. It's at sixty-one-sixty-two to the dollar, fresh record-low territory, having pushed past sixty-one-sixty. Broad dollar strength plus a widening oil-import bill. The BSP can't let the pass-through from a weak peso turn disorderly, so a higher oil price effectively makes the currency defense and the inflation fight the same fight — and both point to keeping policy tight. And the typhoon makes the inflation side worse, right on cue. That's the compounding. The habagat is soaking the western food belt just as the BSP fights a food-driven print — rice and vegetables are the heaviest weights in the basket, so a bad monsoon stretch nudges the very number they're hiking against. Weather reads straight through to monetary policy there in a way it wouldn't in a richer economy. So the clean tailwind we described in June has become a headwind again. In one line: June's relief was oil going their way; July's bind is oil going against them, with a storm on top. The structural offsets still hold — remittances near a tenth of GDP, flattered by the weak peso, plus the dollar-earning BPO sector — but cyclically they're back on the defensive. If crude stays near 80, the August hike is live; if the ceasefire somehow gets restitched, they get their breathing room back. It all keys off Hormuz. Everything keys off Hormuz today. On to vocab. Vocab Of The Day Segment four — vocab. Two Mandarin, two Tagalog, each tied to a story in today's show. Mandarin first, and we do this stretch in Mandarin. 第一个词是高级连词——一旦。(The first word is an advanced connective — yídàn.) 一旦发生某件事，后果就很严重。(Once something happens, the consequences are serious.) 一旦霍尔木兹海峡关闭，油价就会飞涨。(Once the Strait of Hormuz closes, oil prices will soar.) 一旦。所以这是一个关于"假设触发"的连词。(Yídàn. So it's a connective about a hypothetical trigger.) 正是。搭配是"一旦……就……"。(Exactly. The pattern is "yídàn… jiù…") 它和"如果"不一样："如果"很中性，而"一旦"强调一个有严重后果的临界点，语气更书面、更正式。(It differs from rúguǒ: rúguǒ is neutral, while yídàn stresses a threshold with serious consequences — the register is more written, more formal.) 明白了。一旦局势失控，市场就会大跌。(Understood. Once the situation spirals out of control, markets will tumble.) 这就带到第二个词了。(And that brings us to the second word.) 对，第二个词是"局势"。(Right, the second word is júshì — the situation, the state of affairs.) 中东局势非常紧张。(The Middle East situation is very tense.) 这个词几乎总是指政治或军事的局面。(This word almost always refers to a political or military state of affairs.) 局势。跟"形势"有什么区别？(Júshì. How does it differ from xíngshì?) 好问题。"局势"通常指一个具体的、往往紧张的对峙——比如战争局势。(Good question. Júshì usually points to a specific, often tense standoff — a war situation.) "形势"更宽泛，指整体趋势，比如经济形势。(Xíngshì is broader — overall trends, like the economic situation.) 常见搭配：局势紧张、局势稳定、局势升级。(Common collocations: tense situation, stable situation, an escalating situation.) 都是正式、书面的用法。(All formal, written usage.) 一旦局势升级，油价和收益率就一起上涨——今天的整条主线。(Once the situation escalates, oil and yields rise together — today's entire throughline.) 两个词都在新闻里活着。到菲律宾语。(Both words alive in the news. On to Tagalog.) Tagalog naman, at para sa mga katutubong tainga. (Tagalog now, and for native ears.) Unang salita: banta. (First word: banta — a threat, a looming menace.) May banta ng digmaan sa Gitnang Silangan, at may banta rin ng malakas na bagyo sa kanluran ng bansa. (There's a threat of war in the Middle East, and also a threat of a strong storm in the west of the country.) Banta. At ano ang pagkakaiba sa "peligro"? (Banta. And how does it differ from peligro?) Kadalasan pareho silang isinasalin bilang "danger." (They're often both translated as "danger.") Iyan ang tamang ihambing. (That's the right pair to contrast.) Ang banta ay ang nagbabantang panganib — isang babala, isang bagay na papalapit pa lang. (Banta is the looming, threatening danger — a warning, something still approaching.) Ang peligro ay ang aktwal na panganib na kinalalagyan mo na. (Peligro is the actual danger you're already in.) Kaya may banta ng bagyo bago pa ito dumaong; nasa peligro ka na kapag umaapaw na ang tubig. (So there's a threat of a storm before it makes landfall; you're in danger once the water is already rising.) Malinaw. Banta ang paparating; peligro ang narito na. (Clear. Banta is what's coming; peligro is what's here.) Pangalawang salita. Pangalawang salita, diretso mula sa balita ng panahon: hagupit. (Second word, straight from the weather news: hagupit — a lashing, an onslaught.) Ang hagupit ng bagyo at ng habagat ang binabantayan ngayon sa kanluran ng Luzon. (The lashing of the typhoon and the monsoon is what's being watched now in western Luzon.) Hagupit. At ang pagkakaiba sa simpleng "lakas"? (Hagupit. And the difference from simply lakas, strength?) Ang "lakas" ay neutral — ang sukat lang ng puwersa. (Lakas is neutral — just the magnitude of force.) Ang "hagupit" ay ang mismong marahas na pagbugbog na dulot nito — ang latay, ang dagok. (Hagupit is the actual violent battering it delivers — the welt, the blow.) Kaya sinasabi nating hinagupit ng bagyo ang baybayin: hindi lang ito malakas, may tinamaan. (So we say the storm lashed the coast: it isn't just strong, something got struck.) Kaya ngayon: banta ng digmaan, hagupit ng bagyo. Dalawang salita, dalawang panganib, iisang araw. (So today: the threat of war, the lashing of the storm. Two words, two dangers, one day.) Ay, ang ganda ng pagkakabuo. (Beautifully tied together.) Iyan ang ideya. (That's the idea.) Two Mandarin, two Tagalog, all four straight out of today's stories — a threat, a situation, once, and the lash. Art / Pop Segment five — art and pop, quick. It's shaping up to be a diva-heavy summer, and the marquee announcement is Ariana Grande's eighth album, "Petal," out July thirty-first. The headline isn't just the record — it's that it's the first fully independent release of her career. She's described it as something full of life, growing through the cracks of something cold and hard and challenging. The independence angle is the story there — an artist at that scale self-owning a release is a statement about power, not just music. Completely — it's the self-ownership move, and it rhymes with the season. Because the other diva data point is Olivia Rodrigo, whose new album, "You Seem Pretty Sad for a Girl So in Love," dropped last month, debuted at number one on every chart it was eligible for, and moved four hundred eighty-five thousand units in week one — already beating her previous blockbuster. Madonna's got a project queued for the summer too. So what's the connective tissue across all of it? Control. Rodrigo detonating the charts on her own terms, Grande walking out on her own label, Madonna doing Madonna — the through-note is established women running their own rollouts at the top of the industry, and winning the summer doing it. In a culture obsessed with the next algorithm-fed act, the biggest moves are veterans owning their catalogs and their calendars. Artist as label. Good cue for the meme. Trending Meme Segment six — the meme, reported straight. The format climbing the feeds is a song called "Bangladesh," by Ian McConnell. It's a forty-nine-second track that opens like an ordinary relationship complaint — "you never take me to Bangladesh" — and then the demands escalate into pure absurdity: sausages, oil, novels, revenge drinks that make no sense. The joke is the escalation itself, the deadpan slide from a reasonable grievance into nonsense. And it's traveling because it's a template. That's exactly why. It's endlessly re-skinnable — people swap in their own escalating list of impossible demands, and the comedy is in how fast it derails. It sits alongside two other formats having a moment: the "Food Jutsu" bit, an anime-style hand-sign reveal, and a full-blown "hot dog summer" aesthetic anchored to a Jennifer Coolidge audio clip. So the meta-trend under the three of them? Absurdist escalation. Each one takes something mundane — a partner's request, a hand gesture, a hot dog — and cranks it past the point of sense on a dependable curve. It's the internet's current comfort mode: low-stakes, pattern-based, and built to be remixed in an afternoon. No irony about the news cycle, just a reliable machine for turning the ordinary up to absurd. Mundane to absurd, on a timer. Let's end on something good. One Good Thing One good thing to close on. A genuinely delightful one, and it's from the same coral seas we were just talking weather over. Scientists have described a brand-new species of walking shark — Hemiscyllium dudgeonae, Dudgeon's walking shark — off Milne Bay, at the far tip of Papua New Guinea. It's a small reef shark that uses its muscular pectoral fins like legs to walk across the shallows, even between tide pools. It's the first new species in its genus since 2013. A shark that walks. And the locals already knew it? Long before science named it — they call it kadedekedewa, which loosely means the lazy shark, or dog shark, for its little waddling gait. There's a gentle lesson in that: a species can be entirely familiar to the people who live beside it and still be brand-new to the record books. The one shadow is that it's only been found at three sites, so researchers already flag it as at-risk — which makes naming it the first step toward protecting it. And a quieter one for the good-news wire? A nice piece of biology — researchers pinned down a molecular switch that helps explain why exercise keeps ageing muscles healthy. Unglamorous, but the kind of mechanism that turns "stay active" from folk wisdom into a target you can actually design around. A walking shark and a reason to keep moving — I'll take both. From a reef in Papua New Guinea to the machinery of our own muscles — a good note to end on. That's your morning. The war premium is back, yields are climbing, and an AI buildout that just keeps roaring underneath it all — that's the shape of the day. Wherever this finds you, keep an eye on Hormuz, watch the long end of the curve, and we'll be right back here tomorrow. See you then. 一旦 once; in the event that (a hypothetical trigger with serious consequences) the Hormuz-closure tail risk after the Iran ceasefire collapse 局势 situation; state of affairs (usually political or military) the Middle East situation driving the oil and yields reversal banta a threat; a looming menace or warning the Iran escalation and the approaching Super Typhoon Inday hagupit a lashing; the violent onslaught or battering of a storm Typhoon Inday and the enhanced habagat soaking western Luzon

docs.title: Thursday, June 25, 2026
docs.text: Thursday, June 25, 2026 Cold Open Good morning, this is The Morning Commute. Thursday, June twenty-fifth. I'm Sam. And I'm Alex. The throughline today is cheaper oil and lower yields doing the Fed's work for it. The Iran de-escalation is dragging crude into the low seventies, that's pulling Treasury yields down hard, and the disinflation is quietly resetting who leads this market — money rotating out of the crowded megacap-AI trade into rate-sensitives. The twist: the AI buildout underneath is roaring louder than ever, and Micron just proved it after the bell. Headlines Headlines. World desk first. Europe's cooking. It is — France put fifty-four departments under a red heat-wave alert, the top tier, with the UK and Spain issuing red extreme-heat warnings too. Early, punishing, and a grid and public-health story at once: peak cooling demand slams into the transmission constraints we keep flagging in the energy block. And there's a clean line from a heat dome to a power price — a continent-wide cooling spike you absorb at the margin, and the margin is where prices clear. Same physics as the data-center story — the grid doesn't fear the average, it fears the peak. Hold that thought; it comes back hard in segment two. Second world story — Iran, which is really the engine under today's whole show. It is the engine. The June eighteenth framework is holding, Hormuz is normalizing, crude keeps bleeding war premium. But the politics got messy overnight — the US Senate voted to rein in the President's war powers over Iran under the War Powers Act, and Iran and Washington put out flatly contradictory statements on inspections. The IAEA chief says inspectors will visit Iranian sites; Iranian officials say they never agreed to UN inspections. So the market's pricing the de-escalation while the paperwork underneath it is still being argued. How fragile is that? Fragile in the tails, calm in the body. The spot oil move is mostly done — Brent's in the low seventies, the war premium's largely out. What's left is optionality, cheap for a reason: a framework with a Senate fight and an inspections dispute attached, not a signed treaty. Directional read stays disinflationary, but respect the snapback risk. And on the wire, both real but not markets stories — Venezuela hit by two big quakes, magnitude seven-point-two and seven-point-five, and a worsening Ebola outbreak in eastern Congo, over a thousand cases. Good guardrail. Let's do the tape. Market Overview Wednesday's close: S&P off a tenth of a percent to seventy-three fifty-eight, Nasdaq down four-tenths to twenty-five-four-seventy-six, and the Dow up a third of a percent — a hundred eighty-two points — to fifty-one-eight-forty-nine. Third Dow-up, Nasdaq-down split in a week. Rotation, not risk-off. And the tell is what led. The Dow holding green while the Nasdaq bleeds is value and cyclicals taking the baton from megacap tech — not fear, a relay handoff. That's the session. Two drivers. One, megacap tech de-risked into Micron's print — nobody wanted to be max-long the AI complex going into the quarter's most important read. Two, yields fell hard, and that lit a fire under everything rate-sensitive. Quantify the rates move, because it's the biggest input today. The ten-year dropped to four-four-one from four-and-a-half the prior day — nine basis points, and it touched four-thirty-nine intraday. Two-year down to four-eleven, thirty-year to four-eighty-six. A real bond rally, driven by the oil slide plus firmer rate-cut bets into the next Fed meeting. Put real numbers on the crude move, since it's driving everything. Brent settled around seventy-three seventy-four, off more than four percent on the session; US WTI around seventy thirty-four, down a similar clip. Both back to where they sat before the February war premium went in. That's the disinflation engine in two prices — and it's why the ten-year could fall nine basis points without a single soft data point. And you can see the rate trade in the single-stock tape. Vividly. KB Home ripped sixteen-and-a-half percent; the three-times leveraged homebuilder ETF was up almost nineteen. That's not an earnings move — it's a pure duration trade: the ten-year is the affordability lever for the whole homebuilder complex, and a nine-basis-point drop reprices it instantly. And the other side of the rotation — the AI names that got sold. Cerebras was the carnage, down almost twenty percent — an AI-compute de-rating, a richly-valued name taken to the woodshed when positioning unwinds. And Micron itself fell about thirteen percent the prior session going into the print. Which sets up the whole irony of today. Which is that the print blew the doors off. Clean off — full number in US business. The lesson of this tape: the rotation out of megacap AI is a crowding and rates story, not a verdict on the earnings. So how do you frame the Fed setup? Constructive. Falling oil gives the Fed cover, falling yields are already easing financial conditions, the data's cooperating. The hawkish objection is the independence question and cutting into still-warm services inflation — but the energy disinflation impulse is doing the heavy lifting. Before we leave the tape — the positioning read under the rotation? De-grossing in the crowded book, not outright risk-off. Equal-weight has been quietly outperforming cap-weight, advancers roughly matched decliners — under the hood a healthier tape than the Nasdaq headline. The Mag-Seven complex just reminded everyone that concentration cuts both ways: when everyone owns the same eight names, a small de-risk becomes an index-level move. And volatility? Contained — this isn't a fear event, it's a reallocation. The vol you'd actually watch is rates vol, not equity vol, because the whole move is being driven by the bond market. If the ten-year keeps grinding lower, the rotation has further to run; if oil snaps back, it reverses just as fast. US business — lead with the elephant. Micron. U.S. Business Micron, after the close last night, and it's a blowout in the truest sense. Record revenue of forty-one-and-a-half billion dollars — up seventy-four percent quarter-on-quarter and three hundred forty-six percent year-on-year — against a Street looking for about thirty-five billion. Non-GAAP earnings of twenty-five-eleven a share, versus roughly twenty dollars expected, twelve-twenty the prior quarter, and a dollar-ninety-one a year ago. Those aren't beats, they're different orders of magnitude. What drives a number like that? High-bandwidth memory — the stuff that sits next to the AI accelerators. They beat the high end of their own guidance on revenue, gross margin and EPS simultaneously, which almost never happens. HBM-four is already shipping for Nvidia's Vera Rubin platform, all of calendar twenty twenty-six output is contracted, and they announced sixteen strategic customer agreements they say will transform the business model. So the bear case — that this is just another memory cycle run ahead of itself — how does this print speak to it? It speaks directly against it. The classic memory cycle is commodity boom-bust — capacity floods, pricing collapses. What's different now: the demand is contracted years out and the product is differentiated. HBM isn't a commodity DRAM bit, it's a co-designed piece of an accelerator. When your entire annual output is sold forward under price and volume agreements, that's a franchise, not a spot market. The stock fell into the print on positioning; the fundamentals just said the supercycle is intact. The perfect microcosm of the throughline — the AI trade wobbles, the AI buildout roars. The whole show in one stock. Second US story, the other side of the rotation — KB Home. Revenue of one-point-one-one billion, just ahead of the one-point-oh-nine estimate; adjusted earnings of forty-three cents, a hair under the forty-five consensus. They flagged margin pressure and pricing softness — a soft quarter. And the stock went up sixteen-and-a-half percent. Which only makes sense through the rates lens. The market looked straight through the quarter to the ten-year. Completely. Affordability is the entire homebuilder thesis, and it keys off the mortgage rate, which keys off the ten-year. The cleanest expression of the rotation: sell the crowded AI winner, buy the beaten-up duration trade. Two international stories, and they rhyme. International Business & Politics They rhyme on one note: central banks tightening into the oil shock that's now reversing. Start with the Bank of Japan — they hiked to one percent, a seven-to-one vote, the highest policy rate since nineteen ninety-five. Wholesale inflation is accelerating as firms pass through the Middle-East cost shock, and they signaled more. And yet the yen is weaker, not stronger — dollar-yen's near a hundred sixty-two, close to its year high. That's the puzzle. A hiking central bank usually means a firming currency. Rate-differential math. One percent is historic for Japan, but the gap to dollar yields is still enormous, so the carry trade keeps the yen soft even as the BOJ tightens. The risk is non-linear: a hawkish BOJ plus a Fed about to cut is exactly the setup that eventually unwinds yen-funded carry — and that unwind never stays in FX. Second story — the ECB, same script. Same script. The ECB hiked twenty-five basis points earlier this month, deposit rate up to two-and-a-quarter percent — their first hike since twenty twenty-three, reversing eight straight cuts. Same Middle-East inflation impulse, and staff now see euro-area inflation averaging three percent this year. So both Frankfurt and Tokyo tightened to fight an oil shock that's now deflating in real time. The policy lag is the whole risk, isn't it? That's the read — they're fighting last quarter's war. If crude stays in the low seventies, the impulse they hiked against fades and they've tightened into a slowdown. It's the mirror image of the Fed: the Fed gets to cut into falling oil; the ECB and BOJ hiked into rising oil and may have to walk it back. Diverging paths, one common variable — the price of crude. China to close the block. China A flag-planting headline — China retook the top of the world supercomputer rankings, the first time it's led that list since twenty seventeen. Part of the self-reliance push: domestic chips, and an open-weight AI model some are calling another DeepSeek moment. The second-order read is the decoupling scoreboard. Export controls were supposed to cap exactly this — a top ranking is China signaling the controls have a ceiling. That's the lens — sovereignty statement as much as benchmark. But the compute win sits on a two-speed economy. Beijing reaffirmed the roughly five-percent growth target, the trade surplus topped a trillion dollars for the first time — and underneath, domestic demand is soft and the GDP deflator is negative. Which is the debt-deflation trap. Real growth near target masks nominal growth that's barely positive. Exactly — nominal debt service against falling nominal incomes, with property and local-government debt compounding it. Split-screen: world-leading compute and a trillion-dollar surplus on one side, deflation and weak consumption on the other. The technology race they're winning; the demand problem they're not. Does the supercomputer win actually translate into the economy, or is it a showcase? That's the trillion-dollar question, literally. Compute leadership is real strategic capital, but it doesn't fix a property sector in long decline or a household that won't spend. The optimistic case is that AI and advanced manufacturing become the new growth engine as the old property-and-infrastructure model fades. The pessimistic case is world-class supply with no domestic demand to absorb it — which just exports the deflation to everyone else through cheaper goods. Changing gears — the energy desk. Energy / Data Centers / Utilities Segment two — energy, data centers and the grid. This is the AI buildout we keep saying is roaring under the trade. Alex. And there's a hard June marker. PJM — the biggest US grid operator, sixty-seven million people across thirteen states — agreed yesterday to add a new "capacity advisory," a formal warning for when supply could get tight even without extreme weather. That's the tell: they're institutionalizing scarcity that isn't weather-driven. It's AI-load-driven. And that connects back to the heat-wave point — the grid fears the peak, not the average. Except now the peak isn't July air-conditioning, it's a data center. The whole shift. The Department of Energy stood up a simulation platform called Agora to model how hyperscale AI campuses behave on the grid, because they don't behave like normal loads. A GPU training cluster ramps from near-idle to full draw in seconds — so to the grid it's a multi-hundred-megawatt industrial load slamming on instantly. A frequency-stability problem, not just a capacity problem. And the supply response is telling on the energy mix — the clean-energy narrative meets the reliability reality. Look at planned capacity. Natural gas as a share of additions went from about eleven percent in twenty twenty-four to eighteen now; planned non-renewable additions jumped seventy-one percent year-on-year, while renewable growth flattened to about two. That's the market voting for dispatchable power — gas you can ramp on demand — because intermittent supply can't underwrite a load that swings in seconds. Put a number on the scale so people feel it. Global data-center electricity is approaching one thousand fifty terawatt-hours this year. If data centers were a country, they'd be the fifth-largest electricity consumer on earth — between Japan and Russia. That's the demand line that won't quit — and why Micron can sell its whole annual output forward. And the part that turns this into a kitchen-table story. The power bill — the tension with our disinflation throughline. Oil and yields are falling, but electricity is the one price going the other way, because someone pays for the transmission and new generation. When ratepayers see grid upgrades for hyperscaler load on their bills, the cross-subsidization fight gets political fast. Investable surface: regulated utilities with the rate base, IPPs with merchant exposure, the gas-turbine makers — political surface: who eats the cost. There's a water angle to this too, isn't there. A big one. Evaporative cooling can run into the millions of gallons a day per campus, and it collides with exactly the Sun-Belt geographies where the cheap land and the interconnection are — the drought-prone ones. Power, water, and community opposition are converging into the real constraint on the buildout, arguably tighter than chips at this point. And the supply side — how do you actually feed loads growing this fast? Three bottlenecks, all years long: the interconnection queue, transformer lead times, and gas-turbine backlogs. You can announce gigawatts of campuses, but you can't conjure the queue position or the steel. That's why the build is going behind-the-meter — hyperscalers signing dedicated generation, nuclear-restart deals, small-modular-reactor pre-orders that won't deliver this decade. None of it closes a near-term gap, which is why gas and keeping coal online longer keep winning by default. So the clean-energy timeline and the AI timeline are just out of phase. Completely out of phase. The demand is here now; the clean dispatchable supply is years out. That gap is the whole investment thesis — and the whole emissions problem. Power as the counterweight to cheaper crude. Take it to the Philippines. Philippines Segment three — the Philippines. Weather lead, then the macro, and the macro ties right back to oil. Weather first. Typhoon Francisco — international name Mekkhala — briefly punched up to super-typhoon strength and has eased back to a typhoon. Signal Number One is up over Batanes, two towns in mainland Cagayan, and three of the Babuyan islands. The good news: it's not expected to make landfall — it's recurving toward Japan's Ryukyu islands and may exit the Philippine area Thursday evening or Friday. So the headline risk is lower than it looked mid-week. But the real local impact isn't the wind, is it? It's the rain. The bigger story for daily life is the enhanced southwest monsoon — the habagat — that Francisco is pulling in: soaking rain and flooded streets across the west of the country, even with the center offshore. A second system, Higos, is being watched but looks like it'll stay away. A near-miss on the wind, a real soaking from the monsoon. Now the macro bind, because the central bank is in a genuinely tough spot. They are. The BSP raised the policy rate to four-and-three-quarters percent on June eighteenth — the second straight hike. May inflation eased to six-point-eight percent, still miles above the two-to-four-percent target, and they see the full-year average around six-point-four. The driver is imported — oil and fertilizer from the Middle-East conflict feeding straight into food and transport. And they're hiking into a currency problem as much as an inflation problem. The peso's the forcing function. It is — around sixty-one-and-a-half to the dollar, record-low territory, having pushed past sixty-point-seven. Broad dollar strength plus the oil-import bill widening the external position. The BSP can't let the pass-through from a weak peso get disorderly, so they hike — even though the growth side would prefer they didn't. And the typhoon makes the inflation side worse — the monsoon's hitting the food belt. That's the cruel timing. The soaking rain across Luzon lands on rice and vegetable supply just as the BSP is fighting a food-driven print, so a bad monsoon stretch can nudge the very number they're hiking against. Food is the heaviest weight in the Philippine basket, which is why a weather event reads straight through to monetary policy there in a way it wouldn't in a richer economy. And here's where the throughline pays off for them. This is the cleanest tailwind they've had in months. The same oil reversal we opened with takes pressure off the inflation path, the import bill, and the peso, all three at once. If crude stays here, the BSP's job gets materially easier into the back half. And the structural offsets hold: remittances around a tenth of GDP, flattered in peso terms by the weak currency, plus the BPO sector's dollar earnings. And the cost of all this hiking is growth. That's the bind in one line. Every hike to defend the peso and cap imported inflation leans on a consumer already absorbing higher food and fuel. Growth is still seen recovering toward around five percent, but the central bank is effectively choosing currency and price stability over speed — betting the oil relief arrives fast enough to let them stop. If crude cooperates, it's a soft landing; if it snaps back, they're hiking into a slowdown. Cyclically pressured, structurally intact. Beautifully circular. On to vocab. Vocab Of The Day Segment four — vocab. Two Mandarin, two Tagalog, each tied to a story in today's show. Mandarin first. Word one is for the advanced shelf — a connective, the kind of word that separates fluent from textbook. Fǎn'ér. It means "on the contrary," or "instead" — but with a specific flavor: the result is the opposite of what you'd expect. Fǎn'ér. And that's our whole market story today. Perfect for it. You expected yields to rise on the news; fǎn'ér, they fell. Tech was meant to lead; fǎn'ér, the homebuilders ripped. The collocation to lock in is bú dàn méi… fǎn'ér — "not only didn't… but on the contrary…" — where it lives in analyst prose. How's it different from què? I'd reach for què for "but." That's the precise contrast. Què is a mild "however," a gentle pivot. Fǎn'ér is stronger and pointed: it signals an outcome that actively defies the expectation you set up. Què says "but"; fǎn'ér says "and yet the reverse happened." Register leans written and analytical — a finance-desk word. Fǎn'ér. The reverse of what you'd expect. Word two. Word two ties to the central banks and the grid — wěndìng. "Stable," or "stability." Abstract, formal, everywhere in the language of policy and markets. Wěndìng. And the collocation that matters for us? Jiàgé wěndìng — "price stability." Literally the BSP's mandate, the ECB's, the Fed's. And it's portable: grid stability, currency stability, financial stability. Anywhere there's a system you want to hold steady, wěndìng is the word. And the near-synonym to be careful with? Píngwěn. Close, but they split cleanly. Wěndìng is a state — the thing is stable, full stop. Píngwěn is a smooth process — steady, no jolts. The classic is píngwěn zhuólù, a soft landing. So a central bank wants jiàgé wěndìng achieved via a píngwěn process. State versus journey. State versus journey. Clean. On to Tagalog. Tagalog now, and for native ears, so we go for nuance. Word one is straight off the weather page — habagat. The southwest monsoon — the hot, wet, stormy season-wind that Francisco is pulling across the country right now. And the pairing every Filipino feels in their bones is habagat versus amihan. That's the whole nuance. Habagat is the southwest monsoon — humid, rainy, the flooded-street months. Amihan is its opposite number: the northeast monsoon, the cool, dry "ber-months" air everyone loves. Same idea — a prevailing seasonal wind — opposite feeling. One you brace for, one you welcome. Habagat and amihan. And word two. Word two ties to the oil relief — ginhawa. Relief, ease, a sense of well-being. When the import bill loosens and a household can breathe again, that easing is ginhawa. And the native-speaker distinction from luwag? Both get translated as "loosening." Exactly the pair to separate. Luwag is the mechanical loosening — slack in a system, looser policy, a belt with another notch. The cause. Ginhawa is the felt result — the relief, the breathing room you actually experience. Lower oil gives the peso some luwag; what households feel is ginhawa. The rich form, kaginhawaan, means welfare or prosperity outright. Luwag is the slack in the rope; ginhawa is finally being able to breathe. And both live in today's show — the ginhawa of cheaper crude, the habagat over Luzon. Language inside the news. That's the idea. Two Mandarin, two Tagalog, all four from today's stories. Art / Pop Segment five — art and pop, quick. Rock first. The Strokes drop their seventh, "Reality Awaits," tomorrow — reportedly back to the early signature sound, with Wild-West cover art. For a band that spent a decade experimenting, "back to basics" is itself the story. And the pop counterpoint? Charli XCX announced her seventh, "Music, Fashion, Film," for July twenty-fourth — guitar-driven this time, with a cover that puts Martin Scorsese, Marc Jacobs and John Cale in one frame. A statement of intent before a note drops. Anything on screens? Two openings — a new "Masters of the Universe" from Travis Knight, and a "Scary Movie" reboot, both nationwide this month. The thread is how much cultural energy is nostalgia and revival — the same "back to the old sound" instinct running through the music. Same back-to-roots instinct on the Charli record? In a way — going guitar-driven after a run of pop maximalism is its own return to the instrument. Put it next to the Strokes' back-to-basics move and you've got two of the era's defining acts both reaching backward to go forward. The contrarian read: "newness" is getting harder to sell, so even the avant-garde is mining nostalgia. Comfort culture. Good cue for the meme. Trending Meme Segment six — the meme, reported straight. The format eating feeds is "saxophone gets louder." The setup is mundane, then a saxophone blares and the scene cuts to chaos — the joke is life flipping from "this is fine" to "oh no" in a couple of seconds. It's everywhere because it's endlessly remixable and works with the sound off, on the build alone. And the real-world one that crossed over? The "KitKat heist." Over four hundred thousand KitKat bars were genuinely stolen in transit across Europe — a real logistics crime — and the internet turned it into a running gag almost instantly. Real event, meme afterlife. And the meta-trend behind it? The speed of corporate co-option. The "nihilistic penguin" got picked up by big brands — BMW, Lidl — within weeks of breaking, and that speed is now itself the joke: a format is barely born before a car company is posting it. There's also a German audio trend, "du bist gut genug," doing numbers on the short-video apps. The through-note is how fast the feed metabolizes everything now. Brands speed-running the joke. Let's end on something good. One Good Thing One good thing to close on. A big one, and it ties right back to our coastal block. French Polynesia just created a new marine reserve roughly the size of France — around two hundred thousand square miles of ocean around the Austral and Marquesas islands — and banned mining, trawling and industrial fishing across it. Some of the most biodiverse water on the planet, now protected at a genuinely vast scale. After a typhoon segment, a story about coastlines being defended lands differently. It really does. These are the waters that feed island economies and buffer them from exactly the storms we just talked about — and the decision was to wall off the most destructive industrial pressures entirely. One government choosing the long horizon. And one more for the good-news wire? A quieter breakthrough, but a real one — researchers reported a way to break down PFAS, the so-called forever chemicals, using hydrogen radicals from intense ultraviolet light, with no extra chemicals added. They're called forever chemicals because almost nothing severs that bond; a clean, low-input way to do it is the kind of unglamorous science that quietly changes water safety for decades. From an ocean reserve to cleaner water — a good day for the blue parts of the map. We'll take it. That's your morning. Cheaper oil, lower yields, and an AI engine that just won't quit — that's the shape of the day. Wherever this finds you, take it easy, breathe a little ginhawa, and we'll be right back here tomorrow. See you then. 反而 on the contrary; instead (the result is the opposite of what you'd expect) the market rotation — yields fell instead of rising; homebuilders ripped while megacap tech sold off 稳定 stable; stability BSP / ECB / Fed price-stability mandates and grid stability in the energy block habagat the southwest monsoon — the hot, wet, stormy season-wind Typhoon Francisco enhancing the southwest monsoon over Luzon ginhawa relief, ease, a sense of well-being / breathing room the oil reversal easing the Philippines' import bill, inflation path, and peso

docs.title: Tuesday, June 23, 2026
docs.text: Tuesday, June 23, 2026 Cold Open Good morning, this is The Morning Commute. Tuesday, June twenty-third. I'm Sam. And I'm Alex. The throughline today is the Iran de-escalation working its way through every rates and FX market on the board — plus an AI power-demand story that's quietly the biggest capex theme of the decade. Let's get into it. Headlines Headlines. World desk first. Starmer. Starmer's stepping down as Labour leader, staying on as caretaker PM through the contest. Burnham's the bookies' favorite, running from outside the parliamentary party — which is the interesting part, because he'd need a seat. The market question is fiscal continuity. Does a Burnham-led Labour reopen the spending envelope Reeves spent two years defending? That's exactly the tail risk gilts will price. The front end is fine — it's the long end that gets nervous if the market sniffs a looser fiscal stance. Watch the thirty-year and the sterling basis. For now it's a slow-burn re-rating, not a Truss-style gap move, because there's no fiscal event attached yet. So a fade, not a break. Second story — Iran. The June eighteenth MOU is holding. Hormuz reopening, the US lifting the naval blockade, and Bessent's sixty-day general license greenlighting Iranian barrels through August. The war kicked off late February, took Brent to the low eighties and US retail gas to four-fifty-six at the Hormuz peak. But the curve already de-risked most of that. How much war premium is actually left to bleed out? Less than people think — that's the point. The spot move is mostly done; the action now is in the term structure. The back end was pricing a structural supply impairment that's no longer the base case, so you're seeing the curve flatten as the geopolitical premium comes out of the deferreds. The real swing factor is how fast Iranian volumes actually ramp, and whether OPEC-plus accommodates or defends price. And the snapback risk if the MOU breaks? That's the asymmetry — it's a sixty-day license, not a treaty. Optionality is cheap here for a reason. But the directional read is disinflationary, and that's what's driving the rates story we're about to get into. Perfect bridge. Market overview. Market Overview Monday's close: S&P off thirty-seven basis points to seventy-four seventy-three, Nasdaq down one-point-three to twenty-six-one-sixty-six, Dow up a quarter percent. Classic breadth divergence — it was a megacap problem, not a market problem. The tell is the Russell holding while the Nasdaq bled. That's de-grossing in the crowded book, not risk-off. Right. Equal-weight outperformed cap-weight by a wide margin, advancers beat decliners — under the hood it was actually a decent tape. The index headline is just Mag-Seven concentration doing what concentration does. And the catalyst — Alphabet down five on AI talent departures. Is that a real earnings signal or a positioning excuse? Mostly a positioning excuse dressed as a narrative.
