When a name is that consensus-long and richly multiplied, you don't need much — a couple of star researchers walking to a competitor reprices the terminal-value story at the margin. Amazon down almost five, Microsoft off three, Meta down a couple. SpaceX was the real carnage, down sixteen, third straight session — that one's a genuine de-rating, not noise. Where does that leave the Fed setup into Warsh? This is the crux. Warsh is the incoming chair, and his reaction function explicitly discounts supply-side energy shocks — he'd look straight through the oil spike. So with crude now reversing, he's got cover to cut into a market that's already pricing easing. The hawkish objection is the independence question — a Trump pick cutting into a still-warm core — but the data's cooperating. And earnings give him air cover. Twenty-two percent Q2 S&P earnings growth on the FactSet blend, second straight quarter north of twenty. You can tolerate a lot of macro noise when the E in the multiple is compounding like that. Net: constructive, with the caveat that the megacap complex just reminded everyone the beta works both ways. US business. Two deals. U.S. Business Roku-Fox, merger agreement signed the fourteenth. The strategic logic is connected-TV ad inventory plus automatic content recognition data — Roku owns the OS layer and the home screen, Fox brings live sports, news, and Tubi's FAST inventory. So this is a play on the same CTV ad pool The Trade Desk and Amazon are fighting over. The question is whether owning content plus the OS is vertical integration regulators tolerate. That's the whole antitrust question. Roku monetizes the screen regardless of what you watch; bolt on Fox's content and Tubi's reach and you've got a vertically integrated CTV ad machine. Expect a long second request, and watch how they argue the ad market is still fragmented enough to clear it. Deal two — Olin and Huntsman. Merger of equals, twelve-billion-plus North American chemicals platform. Plus Ingredion taking Tate and Lyle for three-point-six. The chemicals tie-up is a classic late-cycle defensive consolidation — chlor-alkali and epoxy have been grinding through a trough with Chinese oversupply capping any pricing recovery. So synergy math over a growth thesis. Is the combined leverage profile the risk, or the antitrust overlap in epoxies? Both get scrutiny, but it's the synergy credibility that makes or breaks the multiple — these are commodity cycles, and the street will haircut the cost-out number until they see it. The broader signal is the one that matters: when organic growth is scarce, boards buy it, and the M&A pipeline keeps building. Two international. International Business & Politics The G10 central bank divergence is the trade. ECB's expected to deliver an insurance hike to lean against the energy pass-through into core, and the BOJ's tilting toward another step in normalization. So you've got Europe and Japan tightening into a Fed that wants to cut. That's straight rate-differential fuel for euro and yen. Does it finally break the dollar smile, or does it just compress the carry? It compresses the carry first — and the more interesting leg is the yen, because a hawkish BOJ plus a dovish Fed is exactly the setup that unwinds yen-funded carry. That's the cross-asset risk nobody's hedged for: a disorderly carry unwind doesn't stay in FX, it shows up in everything that got funded cheaply. Second story. Japan again — the structural one. The megabanks are dropping their long-standing refusal to finance defense, with the policy banks already easing terms. And JAXA put up the H3 number-six on a low-cost configuration, restoring sovereign launch and going after commercial share. Defense plus space — same thesis as the German fiscal turn. Re-armament as a multi-year capex cycle the market's still under-positioned for. Exactly the read. This is balance-sheet capital reallocating toward security as a secular theme, not a headline trade. Money follows geopolitics, and Japan's the cleanest expression of it this cycle. China to close the block. China Two-speed, and the speeds are diverging. Beijing set four-and-a-half to five on growth, PBoC signaling no more cuts this year — they're guarding the rate differential and the currency. Exports up fifteen percent in the first four months, investment firm; consumption soft, property still a drag. But the nominal picture is worse than the real one — the GDP deflator's been negative. Isn't the actual story debt-deflation, not the headline growth print? That's the right lens. Real growth near target masks nominal growth that's barely positive, because the deflator's underwater — PPI's been in deflation for a long stretch. That's the trap: nominal debt service against falling nominal incomes, with LGFV liabilities and property both compounding it. The export strength is partly tariff front-running, which borrows from future quarters. And the one datapoint that captures the model shift. BYD past Tesla as the world's largest EV seller by volume. China's not competing on cost anymore — it's competing on the car. The catch is the domestic price war is torching margins, which is the overcapacity problem in miniature. That's the block. Changing gears. Energy / Data Centers / Utilities Segment two — the energy desk. The AI-power collision. Alex. The June marker is DOE standing up Agora — a simulation platform to model how hyperscale campuses behave on the grid. The reason it matters: these aren't conventional loads. A training cluster swings from near-idle to full draw in seconds, so to the grid it looks like a multi-hundred-megawatt industrial load slamming on instantly. And that volatility is a frequency-stability problem, not just a capacity problem. Steady demand you can plan; sub-second ramps you have to engineer around. Which is the whole reliability headache. PJM is projecting a six-gigawatt shortfall against its reliability requirement in twenty twenty-seven — the market monitor's words were "crisis stage." And the demand curve is eighty gigawatts in twenty twenty-five to a hundred-fifty by twenty-eight. That's a Spain-sized load added in three years. That's why the last PJM capacity auction cleared at records. The interconnection queue is the binding constraint — you can announce gigawatts of campuses, but you can't get the steel and the transformers and the queue position to match. Queue, transformers, and gas turbines — the OEM backlogs run years out now. That's why the build is going behind-the-meter: hyperscalers signing dedicated generation, the nuclear restart deals, the SMR pre-orders that won't deliver this decade. None of it closes a twenty twenty-seven gap, which means gas and keeping retiring coal online longer. And the cost allocation fight — who eats the grid upgrades. That's the political fuse. That's where it turns from a capex story into a ballot-box story. When ratepayers see transmission upgrades for hyperscaler load showing up in their bills, the cross-subsidization question gets political fast. The investable surface: regulated utilities with the rate base, the IPPs with merchant exposure to rising power prices, the gas-turbine OEMs, and the nuclear complex. Multi-year theme, and it touches inflation through the power bill. There's a water angle too — evaporative cooling against drought-region siting. Millions of gallons a day per campus, and it collides with exactly the Sun-Belt geographies where the cheap land and the interconnect are. Power, water, and community opposition are converging into the real constraint on the buildout — arguably tighter than chips at this point. Bring it home. Philippines Segment three — the Philippines. Weather lead, then the macro. Super Typhoon Francisco — local name Mekkhala — holding super-typhoon strength, Signal Number One up across northern Luzon. The economic wrinkle: that's the rice belt, so a hard hit feeds the food-inflation print at precisely the wrong moment. Because the BSP's already boxed in. Walk me through the policy bind. They flipped from easing to hiking — after two-and-a-quarter points of cuts since August twenty twenty-four, they took twenty-five basis points back in April to four-and-a-half, first hike in two years, and flagged more small moves. The driver is imported inflation — oil and fertilizer pushing the projection above the four-percent ceiling for both twenty twenty-six and twenty-seven. So they're hiking into a currency problem as much as an inflation problem. The peso past sixty-point-seven is the real forcing function, isn't it? It is — near a record low, and the BSP can't let the pass-through from a weak peso get disorderly. It's broad dollar strength plus the oil-import bill widening the external position. The silver lining is the one we opened with: the Iran de-escalation pulling crude down takes pressure off both the inflation path and the currency simultaneously. That's the cleanest macro tailwind they've had in months. And the structural offsets — remittances and BPO. Remittances around a tenth of GDP, and a weaker peso actually flatters the converted value, so it's a stabilizer for household consumption even as imports bite. BPO is the dollar earner to watch — and the AI overhang we just discussed is a genuine medium-term risk to the lower end of that stack. Growth's still seen recovering to about five-one this year from four-four. So: cyclically pressured, structurally intact. Quick local color. Batac in Ilocos Norte marks its nineteenth charter anniversary today; Tabuk in Kalinga's got a founding-anniversary holiday — part of a long list the palace declared across nineteen areas this month. Onward. Vocab Of The Day Segment four — vocab. Two Mandarin, two Tagalog. Mandarin first, and slow it down — I'm actually drilling these. Word one ties to the Iran story: negotiation. tán pàn. First syllable rises — tán — like a question. Second falls hard — pàn — like setting your foot down. Together, tán-pàn. tán-pàn. Full sentence: liǎng guó zhèng zài jìn xíng tán pàn — "the two countries are negotiating." The chunk to keep is zhèng zài tán pàn — "currently negotiating." zhèng zài tán pàn. Formal register, or everyday? Newsroom and boardroom register. For haggling a price you'd reach for tǎo jià huán jià. Say the chunk once more. zhèng zài tán pàn. Got it. Word two ties to the energy block: power grid. diàn wǎng. diàn falls — fourth tone — and it's electricity, the same diàn in diàn nǎo, "electric brain," computer. wǎng dips and rises — third tone — net or web, same wǎng as the internet. diàn wǎng — electric web. The grid. diàn wǎng. Electric web. That image makes it stick. Sentence, folding in today's story: shù jù zhōng xīn gěi diàn wǎng dài lái hěn dà yā lì — "data centers put enormous pressure on the power grid." Quick tone check before we leave Mandarin — I keep collapsing the falling and the dipping. Drill the contrast directly: pàn is fourth tone, falling, decisive — pàn. wǎng is third tone, dipping then rising — wǎng. Back to back: pàn, wǎng. That one contrast carries most of your error rate. pàn... wǎng. Falling, dipping. Clean. Now Tagalog — and for native speakers, so we go for nuance, not glossary. Word one: alimpuyo. ah-leem-POO-yoh. A whirl, a vortex — of wind, water, or feeling. Alimpuyo ng hangin, whirlwind; alimpuyo ng damdamin, turmoil of emotion. The native-speaker distinction from ipo-ipo? Weight and register. Ipo-ipo is the small, almost playful whirlwind in the yard. Alimpuyo is literary, forceful, churning — the word for a super typhoon's fury, or a chaotic market session. In a sentence: sa gitna ng alimpuyo ng merkado, kailangan ng malamig na ulo — "in the middle of the market's turbulence, keep a cool head." And word two. sapantaha. sah-pahn-TAH-hah. A conjecture, a reasoned supposition — a view formed before the facts are in. Against haka-haka, which leans toward idle, unfounded speculation, sapantaha is dignified — a considered hypothesis. Sapantaha lang ito, ngunit malamang tama — "this is only a conjecture, but it's likely correct." One a hunch, the other a thesis. And both live in today's show — the alimpuyo of the typhoon and the tape, the sapantaha behind every rate-cut bet. Language inside the news. That's the idea. Art / Pop Segment five — art and pop, quick. Rock revival — The Strokes drop their seventh, "Reality Awaits," the twenty-sixth, reportedly back to the early sound. And Charli XCX, off scoring the new "Wuthering Heights," announced "Music, Fashion, Film" for late July. Album-of-the-year conversation? BTS's "Arirang" comeback is the headline, leaning into Korean roots — plus strong returns from Harry Styles, Robyn, Noah Kahan, Olivia Rodrigo. July's stacked: Madonna, Ariana Grande, Tyla all landing. And the screen side? The telling one — "Mandalorian and Grogu" underperforming the usual Star Wars curve, to the point the Backrooms film, born from an internet horror meme, out-earned it. Cultural energy migrating from legacy IP to internet-native stories. The slow-burn structural story is the Taylor Swift–Disney tie-up reshaping how one catalog drives a whole ecosystem. Internet-native culture eating the old guard. Good cue for the last segment. Trending Meme Segment six — the meme. The format eating feeds is the "supportive, disappointed, sarcastic, flirty" challenge — one line delivered four ways, the whole gag in how far the meaning bends on tone alone. It's everywhere because it's endlessly remixable, and it's the rare format that works without sound. And the meta-trend behind it? The "Great Meme Reset of twenty twenty-six" — teenagers organizing to ditch AI brain-rot and bring back the classics. The kids unionizing against the algorithm. Also live: "New York is Gotham" on every gritty subway clip, and the Spurs–Knicks Finals eating the timeline meme by meme. Rooting for the kids. One Good Thing And before we let you drive — one good thing to end on. A genuinely hopeful one. A major global study out this month finds mangrove forests are now gaining faster than they're being lost — the first time that trend has flipped in sixteen years, as restoration projects and tougher protections finally start to bite. And the reason it lands today: mangroves are the cheapest storm barrier coastal communities have. After our typhoon block, that one's close to home. Nature quietly winning one. Anything else on the good-news wire? New Zealand's kākāpō — the flightless night parrot, down to two hundred thirty-five adults — just posted its best breeding season on record: over a hundred chicks this year, against an old high of eighty-five. For one of the rarest birds on earth, that's an enormous margin. A rebounding coastline and a hundred new parrots. We'll take it. That's your commute. Whether it's an alimpuyo out there or smooth sailing — — pace yourself, hydrate, back here tomorrow. Drive safe. 谈判 negotiation; to negotiate Iran de-escalation talks 电网 power grid (lit. 'electric net') AI data-center power demand alimpuyo a whirl, vortex, or turbulent churning — of wind, water, or emotion Super Typhoon Francisco / the market tape sapantaha a conjecture; a reasoned supposition formed before the facts are in rate-cut expectations / Fed setup
